subject: ap-psychology
title: Research Methods — Sleep, Memory, and Cognitive Performance (AAQ)
stimulus:
Introduction
This study investigated the effect of social media use before sleep on academic performance in college students.
Participants
A total of 154 undergraduate students at a mid-sized public university were recruited via flyers posted around campus. Participants received extra credit in their introductory psychology course. The sample was 58% women and 42% men, with ages ranging from 18 to 24 years (mean age = 19.7, standard deviation = 1.4). Racial composition was reported as: 48% White/European American, 22% Hispanic/Latino, 16% Black/African American, 9% Asian/Asian American, and 5% Other.
Method
Participants were randomly assigned to one of two conditions for a four-week period. In the restricted social media condition, participants were instructed to stop using all social media platforms (Instagram, TikTok, Snapchat, Twitter/X) at least 90 minutes before their intended bedtime. In the unrestricted social media condition, participants continued their normal social media habits with no restrictions. All participants wore actigraphy wristbands that automatically recorded sleep duration and estimated sleep quality each night throughout the study. Compliance in the restricted condition was monitored via a brief daily diary entry submitted through a secure online form.
At the end of four weeks, all participants completed a standardized cognitive performance battery that measured attention, working memory capacity (via n-back task), and processing speed. Participants also self-reported their cumulative GPA from the most recently completed academic semester.
Results and Discussion
Data from 14 participants were excluded due to incomplete actigraphy data or fewer than 75% diary compliance entries, leaving a final analyzed sample of 140 participants (70 per condition). The researchers hypothesized that restricting social media before sleep would improve sleep duration, which in turn would improve cognitive performance.

Mean Scores by Condition

| Measure | Restricted Condition | Unrestricted Condition |
|---|---|---|
| Average nightly sleep (hours) | 7.4 | 6.6 |
| Working memory n-back accuracy (%) | 81 | 73 |
| Processing speed (ms, lower = faster) | 312 | 358 |
| Self-reported GPA | 3.31 | 3.18 |

Participants in the restricted condition reported significantly higher sleep duration and outperformed those in the unrestricted condition on all cognitive measures. The researchers concluded that limiting social media exposure before sleep may benefit academic performance by improving sleep quality and increasing available cognitive resources the following day.

parts.prompt: Identify the research method used in the study.

scoring_points.description: Accurately identifies the research method as an experiment, referencing random assignment or manipulation of an independent variable.

required_elements: The researchers used an experiment because participants were randomly assigned to the restricted or unrestricted social media conditions.
alternatives.correct_example: The researchers used an experiment because participants were randomly assigned to the restricted or unrestricted social media conditions.

required_elements: The study was an experiment because an independent variable (social media restriction) was manipulated.
alternatives.correct_example: The study was an experiment because an independent variable (social media restriction) was manipulated.

wrong_examples: The researchers used a survey. [Incorrect method identification.]
The researchers conducted a correlational study. [Incorrect — random assignment makes it an experiment.]
common_traps: Confusing an experiment with a correlational study
Identifying the survey or diary as the research method rather than the overall experimental design

parts.prompt: State the operational definition of sleep quality as used in the study.

scoring_points.description: States a measurable or quantifiable definition of sleep quality as operationalized in the study — specifically nightly sleep duration and/or estimated sleep quality as recorded by the actigraphy wristband.

required_elements: Sleep was measured by actigraphy wristbands that recorded sleep duration each night.
alternatives.correct_example: Sleep was measured by actigraphy wristbands that recorded sleep duration each night.

required_elements: The operational definition of sleep quality is the data automatically collected by the wristband sensors worn by participants.
alternatives.correct_example: The operational definition of sleep quality is the data automatically collected by the wristband sensors worn by participants.

wrong_examples: Sleep quality means how well someone sleeps. [Too vague — does not reference the operationalization used in the study.]
Sleep quality was measured by the GPA data. [Incorrect — GPA was a separate measure, not the operational definition of sleep quality.]
common_traps: Providing a conceptual definition of sleep quality instead of the study's specific operationalization
Confusing sleep quality with another dependent variable in the study

parts.prompt: Describe what the data in the table indicate about the difference in working memory accuracy between the two conditions.

scoring_points.description: Accurately describes what the mean scores indicate — that participants in the restricted social media condition had higher working memory n-back accuracy (81%) compared to those in the unrestricted condition (73%), and explains what this difference indicates in the context of the study.

required_elements: The restricted group performed better on the working memory task, scoring 8 percentage points higher than the unrestricted group.
alternatives.correct_example: The restricted group performed better on the working memory task, scoring 8 percentage points higher than the unrestricted group.

required_elements: Participants who stopped social media before bed were more accurate on the n-back task (81%) than those with no restrictions (73%), indicating that sleep restriction improved working memory.
alternatives.correct_example: Participants who stopped social media before bed were more accurate on the n-back task (81%) than those with no restrictions (73%), indicating that sleep restriction improved working memory.

wrong_examples: The restricted group scored 81%. [States only the value without indicating what that means in the study.]
The mean of the unrestricted group is lower. [Does not explain what the difference indicates in context.]
common_traps: Reporting one mean without comparing the groups or explaining the implication
Describing which group scored higher without explaining what this indicates about the effect of the manipulation

parts.prompt: Identify at least one ethical guideline that the researchers followed in conducting this study.

scoring_points.description: Accurately identifies at least one ethical guideline actually applied by the researchers in the study — must be grounded in evidence from the study description.

required_elements: The researchers protected participant confidentiality by collecting diary entries through a secure online form.
alternatives.correct_example: The researchers protected participant confidentiality by collecting diary entries through a secure online form.

required_elements: Participants gave informed consent before joining the study.
alternatives.correct_example: Participants gave informed consent before joining the study.

required_elements: Participants received extra credit as disclosed compensation, consistent with the ethical principle of voluntary participation with incentives.
alternatives.correct_example: Participants received extra credit as disclosed compensation, consistent with the ethical principle of voluntary participation with incentives.

wrong_examples: The researchers should have debriefed participants at the end of the study. [Describes what should have happened, not an ethical guideline actually applied.]
The researchers acted ethically. [Too vague — does not identify a specific guideline.]
common_traps: Identifying an ethical guideline not supported by evidence in the study description
Suggesting an ethical flaw instead of identifying a guideline that was followed

parts.prompt: Explain the extent to which the research findings may or may not be generalizable, using specific and relevant evidence from the study.
parts.scoring_notes: If students assert the study is 'partly' generalizable, their evidence must support both a reason it is generalizable and a reason it is not to earn the point.

scoring_points.description: Explains the extent to which the study is generalizable with a specific claim about a population AND specific supporting evidence referencing participant variables from the study.

required_elements: The study may not be generalizable beyond college students because participants were all undergraduates recruited at a single mid-sized public university.
alternatives.correct_example: The study may not be generalizable beyond college students because participants were all undergraduates recruited at a single mid-sized public university.

required_elements: The findings may be generalizable to college-aged young adults because the sample included a racially diverse group of participants.
alternatives.correct_example: The findings may be generalizable to college-aged young adults because the sample included a racially diverse group of participants.

required_elements: The results may not apply to older adults or non-students because all participants were 18–24 years old.
alternatives.correct_example: The results may not apply to older adults or non-students because all participants were 18–24 years old.

wrong_examples: The study is generalizable. [No evidence provided.]
The study used a good sample size. [No specific claim about to whom findings generalize.]
common_traps: Asserting generalizability without referencing specific participant characteristics as evidence
Claiming partial generalizability without addressing both directions

parts.prompt: Explain how at least one of the research findings supports or refutes the hypothesis that restricting social media before sleep improves cognitive performance.
parts.scoring_notes: Partial credit (1 pt) is awarded for using evidence from the study without fully explaining how the finding supports or refutes the hypothesis, or for explaining without using a specific finding. Full credit (2 pts) requires using a specific, accurately interpreted finding AND explaining how it supports or refutes the hypothesis.

scoring_points.description: 1 point (partial): Uses specific evidence from the study OR accurately explains the relationship between findings and the hypothesis, but not both. For example, cites a specific result without explaining its connection, or explains the connection without citing a specific result.

required_elements: Uses a result from the table (e.g., working memory scores, processing speed, or GPA) in relation to the hypothesis without a full explanation of the connection.
alternatives.correct_example: The restricted condition had higher working memory accuracy (81% vs. 73%), which seems to support the hypothesis.

wrong_examples: The hypothesis was supported. [No evidence cited and no explanation.]
common_traps: Citing a result without explaining how it connects to the hypothesis
Explaining a connection without referencing specific data from the study

scoring_points.description: 1 point (full): Uses a specific, accurately interpreted finding from the study AND explains how that finding supports or refutes the hypothesis that social media restriction before sleep improves cognitive performance.

required_elements: The restricted condition showed higher working memory accuracy (81% vs. 73%) and faster processing speed (312ms vs. 358ms), which supports the hypothesis because these differences indicate better cognitive performance when social media was restricted before sleep.
alternatives.correct_example: The restricted condition showed higher working memory accuracy (81% vs. 73%) and faster processing speed (312ms vs. 358ms), which supports the hypothesis because these differences indicate better cognitive performance when social media was restricted before sleep.

required_elements: The restricted group averaged 7.4 hours of sleep versus 6.6 hours in the unrestricted group, and also scored higher on cognitive measures — supporting the hypothesis that reduced social media use improves sleep, which in turn benefits cognitive performance.
alternatives.correct_example: The restricted group averaged 7.4 hours of sleep versus 6.6 hours in the unrestricted group, and also scored higher on cognitive measures — supporting the hypothesis that reduced social media use improves sleep, which in turn benefits cognitive performance.

wrong_examples: The hypothesis was supported because the restricted condition did better. [Too vague — no specific data cited and no explanation of the mechanism.]
common_traps: Accurately citing data but not explaining how the finding connects to the hypothesis
Misinterpreting the direction of the results (e.g., treating lower processing speed ms as worse when lower = faster)
